primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 1 with gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome) exacerbation and concurrent acute renal colic

comorbidities.name: Gastrinoma / Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (refractory peptic ulcer disease with secretory diarrhoea)
Primary hyperparathyroidism with recurrent nephrolithiasis / renal colic
Pituitary adenoma (family/patient features — bitemporal visual field defect suggests mass effect)

pathophysiology_summary: The unifying explanation is a Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome (features here fit MEN1: gastrinoma + primary hyperparathyroidism + pituitary adenoma). A pancreatic/duodenal gastrinoma autonomously secretes gastrin, driving extreme gastric acid hypersecretion that produces severe, therapy-resistant peptic ulceration (unresponsive even to maximum-dose proton pump inhibition) and secretory watery diarrhoea (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome). Concurrently, parathyroid hyperplasia/adenoma causes primary hyperparathyroidism and hypercalcaemia, promoting recurrent calcium renal stones and the current renal colic. A pituitary adenoma explains the visual-field involvement and the family/sibling history. The acute danger is not the syndrome label but its immediate complications: an unrelenting, refractory ulcer at high risk of haemorrhage or perforation, and a potentially obstructed/infected renal tract — either of which can become rapidly life-threatening. Ongoing NSAID (ibuprofen) use compounds the ulcer-perforation risk. Episodic headache with profuse sweating and an elevated blood pressure raise the additional possibility of catecholamine excess (phaeochromocytoma), which must be excluded before any procedure. Definitive diagnosis requires serum gastrin, calcium and PTH, plasma/urinary metanephrines, cross-sectional imaging and genetic testing — none obtainable via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe burning epigastric pain radiating to the back, on a background of long-standing ulcers.
Colicky left flank pain radiating to the groin in a patient with recurrent stones.
Severe unremitting ulcer pain carries a real risk of perforation; must be actively excluded.
Epigastric pain radiating to the back — but does not explain flank colic, diarrhoea or family history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening it is reasonable to hold two separate acute problems; the AI should NOT prematurely close on an isolated ulcer or an isolated stone.

differential.diagnosis: gastrinoma / Zollinger-Ellison syndrome within a MEN syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Maximum-dose PPI failure + chronic secretory diarrhoea + weight loss + strong endocrine-tumour family history unify the picture.

differential.diagnosis: primary hyperparathyroidism causing calcium renal stones
differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent stones plus a MEN background point to hypercalcaemia from hyperparathyroidism.

differential.diagnosis: isolated H. pylori / acid-related peptic ulcer
differential.evidence_basis: Absolute failure of maximum-dose PPI plus secretory diarrhoea and family history make a simple acid-related ulcer implausible as the whole story.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The discriminating feature is PPI-refractoriness; recognising it reframes the case from 'bad ulcer' to 'hormone-driven acid hypersecretion'.

differential.diagnosis: MEN syndrome (suspected MEN1) with acute gastrinoma exacerbation + renal colic
differential.evidence_basis: Multi-system endocrine involvement with a dual-site acute pain crisis; high perforation/haemorrhage and obstruction/infection risk.
Episodic headache, profuse diaphoresis and hypertension — must be excluded before any procedure/anaesthesia.
An obstructing stone with infection can decompensate to sepsis quickly; fever/rigors would signal it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Screening should surface the perforation, haemorrhage, urosepsis and catecholamine-crisis threats; the correct action is escalation, not further home management.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected MEN1 with gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison) exacerbation and concurrent acute renal colic
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be confirmed remotely; requires serum gastrin/calcium/PTH, metanephrines, imaging and genetic testing. The telehealth job is recognition + NSAID cessation + emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A confident specific diagnosis is NOT expected via telehealth; a confident recognition of a dangerous multi-system pattern requiring urgent hospital work-up IS.

red_flags.description: Severe, unrelenting peptic-ulcer pain refractory to maximum-dose PPI — high risk of ulcer haemorrhage or perforation
red_flags.clinical_significance: A refractory, escalating ulcer can bleed or perforate; perforation causes peritonitis and sepsis and is rapidly life-threatening.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal gastrointestinal haemorrhage or perforation with peritonitis.

red_flags.description: Ongoing NSAID (ibuprofen) use in the presence of an active, severe ulcer
red_flags.clinical_significance: NSAIDs directly erode gastric mucosa and multiply perforation/bleeding risk; cessation is an immediate, patient-doable safety action.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued NSAID use precipitates ulcer perforation or bleeding.

red_flags.description: Concurrent acute renal colic with risk of an obstructed, infected renal tract
red_flags.clinical_significance: An obstructing stone with superimposed infection can progress to urosepsis; fever/rigors are the escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated obstructed pyelonephritis progressing to septic shock.

red_flags.description: Episodic severe headache with profuse diaphoresis and elevated blood pressure — possible phaeochromocytoma
red_flags.clinical_significance: Unrecognised catecholamine excess risks a hypertensive/catecholamine crisis, especially around procedures or anaesthesia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Hypertensive crisis, arrhythmia or stroke during an unplanned intervention.

red_flags.description: Constitutional decline — chronic secretory diarrhoea and unintentional weight loss on a strong endocrine-tumour family background
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals a systemic neoplastic process rather than a benign local ulcer; mandates comprehensive endocrine work-up.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed underlying neoplastic syndrome and delayed definitive treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating this as a routine acid-related ulcer and advising a higher PPI dose or an H. pylori test — the maximum-dose PPI failure is precisely what a simple ulcer cannot explain.
Framing the flank pain as a self-limiting kidney stone and reassuring, missing the obstruction/infection risk and the unifying endocrine syndrome.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on a single system
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Failing to connect the dual-site pain, secretory diarrhoea, weight loss and family history into one multi-system neoplastic pattern.
Not asking about anti-inflammatory use and therefore missing the single most reversible driver of perforation risk.
Overlooking episodic headache/sweating/hypertension as a possible phaeochromocytoma that must be excluded before any procedure.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Feature-set inconsistency to flag on review
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Mucosal neuromas are classically a MEN2B feature while the note labels the syndrome MEN1 (gastrinoma + hyperparathyroidism + pituitary). The syndrome subtype is unconfirmable remotely; the telehealth task is escalation, and the subtype is left to tertiary work-up. Flagged for clinician review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation findings (epigastric tenderness, rebound, board-like rigidity)
Blood pressure and heart rate without a validated home device
Serum gastrin, calcium and PTH; plasma/urinary metanephrines
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an unexcludable acute surgical/endocrine emergency; instruct immediate cessation of all NSAIDs; keep nil by mouth; arrange emergency ambulance transfer (000) to a tertiary hospital for CT, parenteral analgesia and gastroenterology/urology/endocrine review. The AI Doctor does not administer parenteral analgesia or prescribe an oral analgesia dose.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The specific syndrome cannot and should not be pinned down remotely. The real risk is UNDER-action — anchoring on a simple ulcer or a simple stone, up-titrating the PPI, or advising home analgesia instead of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG) — NSAID-associated and refractory peptic ulcer disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Refractory ulceration despite maximal acid suppression warrants specialist investigation for hypersecretory states (e.g. gastrinoma); cease ulcerogenic NSAIDs.

guidelines_referenced.reference: MEN1 Clinical Practice Guidelines (Thakker et al., 2012)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Gastrinoma, primary hyperparathyroidism and pituitary adenoma constitute the classic MEN1 triad; biochemical and genetic confirmation is required and first-degree relatives should be screened.
